attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old patient in post-anaesthesia recovery unit (PARU) is complaining of severe pruritus following his proctosigmoidectomy surgery. He does not have a rash.

At the start of the procedure, he received intrathecal morphine for postoperative analgesia. To improve his pruritus, he was ordered a dose of IV promethazine by one of the surgical team doctors, as they thought he was having a mild allergic reaction to the morphine. 5 minutes after this, the nurse looking after him reports that he is becoming difficult to rouse.

Upon assessment, his sedation score is 3. Examination reveals respiratory rate of 12 breaths/min, blood pressure 125/90 mmHg, temperature 37.2C and Sp02 99% on 4 L/min oxygen. Abdominal, cardiovascular, and respiratory examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following is the most appropriate management option?
options: Supportive management and more frequent vital signs monitoring
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of severe opioid-induced ventilatory impairment (OIVI) following administration of promethazine - a sedating antihistamine - in a patient who has also been given an opioid. The presentation of skin itchiness without a rash means it is likely opioid-induced pruritus. This is more common in patients given intrathecal morphine compared with systemic morphine, especially in younger patients.  It is important to note that promethazine is not appropriate management of opioid-induced pruritus, which is unrelated to histamine release, meaning an antihistamine such as promethazine would be ineffective. In addition, promethazine and other sedating antihistamines increase the risk of OIVI.

Patients who are receiving any opioid should be closely monitored with regular documentation of their sedation score. Increasing sedation is a more accurate clinical indicator of OIVI than respiratory rate, as patients with severe OIVI can have a respiratory rate is that is within ‘acceptable limits.' Therefore, it is important to monitor patients’ sedation scores to screen for OIVI and allow early and appropriate interventions.

Sedation scores and suggested action:

  • 0   Wide awake and alert = Continue routine monitoring

  • 1   Easy to rouse = Continue routine monitoring

  • 2   Easy to rouse but unable to stay awake = Withhold opioid until the patient is more awake; reduce subsequent opioid dose regardless of pain score; increase frequency of monitoring; notify medical officers; and consider opioid non-responsive pain.

  • 3   Difficult to rouse/unconscious = Withhold opioid; administer naloxone (IV/subcut/IM); initiate emergency medical response; and provide supportive care as required (eg. supplemental oxygen). If continuing opioids, reduce the dose.

The general principle is that clinicians should aim for a sedation score of <2. In this patient, with a sedation score of 3, administering naloxone is the most appropriate management plan. Naloxone is an opioid antagonist that rapidly reduces the effects of opioid medications by displacement of the medication at the mu opioid receptor. Naloxone can also improve symptoms of opioid-induced pruritus resulting from intrathecal morphine, but smaller doses are commonly used as it risks reversal of the analgesia.

Immediate intubation and ventilation is not indicated at this stage. Naloxone is the gold-standard initial treatment for severe OIVI.

Supportive management and more frequent monitoring alone is not appropriate in patients with severe OIVI and requires prompt naloxone injection to prevent morbidity and mortality.

The use of adrenaline would be the correct choice if the patient had had an anaphylactic reaction which is not the case.

Administer promethazine antidote is also incorrect, given there is currently no promethazine antidote.
learningPoints: Increasing sedation is a more reliable indicator of opioid-induced ventilatory impairment (OIVI) than respiratory rate or SpO2 levels.
Administer naloxone to patients with a sedation score of 3
An antihistamine is not an appropriate management plan of opioid-induced pruritus. Sedative antihistamines in particular increase the risk of OIVI when patients have also been given an opioid
references: Macintyre P, Schug A. Acute Pain Medicine: a Practical Guide 5e. Boca Raton: CRC Press;2021. Viewed July 2022

Australian and New Zealand College of Anaesthetists and Faculty of Pain Medicine. PS41(G) Position statement on acute pain management. From https://www.anzca.edu.au/getattachment/558316c5-ea93-457c-b51f-d57556b0ffa7/PS41(G)-Position-statement-on-acute-pain-management-PILOT-(PS41)#page=  Published 2022.
National Prescribing Service. Safe use of opioids in acute pain. From https://www.nps.org.au/safe-use-of-opioids-in-acute-pain . Published 2022. Accessed December 2022.
